items.correct: Holding minimal stock and receiving inputs only when needed for production — reducing storage costs but increasing vulnerability to supply disruption.
items.misconception: Just-in-time means delivering products to customers exactly on time.
items.random_wrong: Just-in-time means finishing work right before the deadline.
items.why: JIT is about INPUT stock to production, not output delivery to customers. Students misread the term as a delivery promise; it is an internal stock control philosophy.

items.correct: Competitive rivalry, threat of new entrants, threat of substitutes, bargaining power of suppliers, bargaining power of buyers — together determining industry attractiveness and potential profitability.
items.misconception: Porter's Five Forces are five business strategies a firm should choose between.
items.random_wrong: Porter's Five Forces are five physical security measures for a factory.
items.why: Five Forces analyse INDUSTRY structure, not firm strategy choices (that's Porter's generic strategies, a different framework). Students conflate the two Porter models.

items.q: Which sector does manufacturing belong to?
items.correct: The secondary sector — which transforms raw materials from the primary sector (farming, mining) into finished goods.
items.misconception: Manufacturing is in the primary sector because it makes things.
items.random_wrong: Manufacturing is in the tertiary sector along with services.
items.why: Primary = extracting raw materials; secondary = transforming them; tertiary = services. 'Making things' suggests primary to students, but only extraction is primary — building/processing is secondary.

items.q: Job enrichment is BEST described as.
items.correct: Adding meaning and responsibility to a role
items.why: It's Adding meaning and responsibility to a role. “Pay rise” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A team given autonomy and praise is being managed in line.
items.why: It's Theory Y. “Theory X” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Worker on £400/week + 10% bonus on sales of £1,500. What is total pay, in £?
items.why: The real figure is 550. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
